terms.def: Current market price for immediate delivery; commodity futures converge to spot at expiry.
Futures price > spot price; typical for financial assets and storable commodities with storage costs.
Futures price < spot price; common for commodities with high convenience yield or supply shortages.
P&L from rolling futures; positive in backwardation (sell high, buy lower), negative in contango.
Benefit of holding physical commodity (e.g. oil as buffer stock); implied from futures pricing: F = S·e^((r−y)T).
Investable basket of commodity futures (e.g. GSCI, BCOM); returns = spot return + roll yield + collateral yield.

eli3: Commodities are raw materials: energy (crude oil, natural gas), metals (gold, silver, copper, aluminium), agriculture (wheat, corn, soybeans, coffee). As investments, commodities are typically accessed through futures contracts rather than physical ownership. Returns come from price changes, roll yield (rolling futures contracts), and collateral yield (interest on the cash held as margin).

Commodities are unique because their prices are determined by physical supply and demand — harvest yields, OPEC production decisions, inventory levels — not just financial flows. This makes commodities behave differently from stocks and bonds, especially during inflationary periods when commodity prices tend to rise sharply.

Gold is special among commodities: it's primarily a monetary metal (small industrial use), held as a store of value and hedge against tail risks. It tends to rise during financial crises, high inflation, and currency debasement — but earns no income (no dividends or coupons), so it has an opportunity cost compared to other assets.

whenWhy.title: Commodity futures structure: spot, futures, roll yield
whenWhy.body: Commodity return decomposition: Total Return = Spot Return + Roll Return + Collateral Return. (1) Spot return: change in the underlying commodity's spot price. Driven by supply/demand fundamentals; (2) Roll return (roll yield): when the futures contract nears expiry, it must be rolled into the next contract. In contango (futures price > spot): rolling costs money — you sell the expiring contract and buy a higher-priced future contract. Negative roll yield; In backwardation (futures price < spot): rolling earns money — you sell expensive near-contract and buy cheaper far-contract. Positive roll yield; (3) Collateral return: commodity futures require only a margin deposit (typically 5-10% of contract value). The remaining capital earns the risk-free rate. Historically, roll return has been the most variable component. S&P GSCI commodity index: heavily weighted to energy (WTI crude oil ~30%); suffered from persistent contango in energy futures 2005-2020 → severe negative roll yield. Bloomberg Commodity Index: more diversified, capped sectors, less negative roll. Theories of normal backwardation (Keynes 1930): commodity producers hedge by selling futures; they pay a risk premium to speculators who take the long side → futures below expected spot → positive risk premium for commodity longs.

whenWhy.title: Commodities as portfolio diversifiers
whenWhy.body: Portfolio roles of commodities: (1) Inflation hedge: commodity prices are a direct input to CPI. Energy (~7% of CPI), food (~14% of CPI): a 10% rise in oil prices adds ~0.7% to headline CPI. Commodity futures inflation beta ≈ 7-14 (highly responsive to unexpected inflation). Stocks: inflation beta ≈ -0.5 to 0. Commodities are the strongest inflation hedge in the short run; (2) Diversification: commodity returns have low correlation with equities (~0.1-0.3) and bonds (~0.0-0.1) over long periods. However, in liquidity crises (2008, 2020), commodity prices also fell sharply as risk-off sentiment dominated; (3) Tail risk hedge: gold specifically provides crisis protection. Gold correlation with equities in normal times ≈ -0.1; in financial crises ≈ -0.3 to -0.5 (safe haven buying); (4) Supply shock buffer: commodity exposure helps when supply shocks hit the economy. 1973-74 oil shock: equity portfolios without commodity exposure lost 40%; commodity exposure was strongly positive. 2022 Ukraine war: energy and agriculture commodities surged while financial assets fell — commodity long positions provided critical offsets.

whenWhy.title: Commodity investment vehicles
whenWhy.body: Ways to invest in commodities: (1) Commodity futures directly: managed futures or commodity trading advisors (CTAs). Most direct exposure; allows short positions; requires futures expertise; (2) Commodity ETFs/ETNs: (a) Physically-backed (gold ETFs: GLD, IAU; silver ETF: SLV): track spot price by holding physical metal. No roll cost but storage costs for metals; (b) Futures-based ETF (USO for oil, DJP for diversified): tracks nearby futures contracts, incurs roll cost. USO suffered heavily from contango in 2020 (oil went negative); (3) Commodity index funds: long-only diversified commodity exposure (S&P GSCI, Bloomberg Commodity Index). Accessible, diversified, liquid; (4) Commodity producer equities: mining stocks (Barrick Gold), oil majors (ExxonMobil). Indirect exposure — returns also depend on equity market and company-specific factors; (5) Physical commodity ownership: gold bars, copper stored in LME warehouses. Highest purity of exposure; high storage/insurance costs; illiquid. For institutional investors: commodity index swaps (OTC) or listed futures through a managed account. Most cost-efficient for large allocations.

deepDiveIntro: Commodity price dynamics: supply and demand balance with inventories as buffer. Price = f(demand, supply, inventories). Hotelling's Rule (1931): for a non-renewable resource (oil, metals) with no extraction cost: price should rise at the risk-free rate over time (the resource becomes more valuable as it depletes). In practice: technological change (shale revolution), new discoveries, and demand shifts dominate over Hotelling dynamics. Inventory model (Working 1949): Futures price = Spot × (1 + r) × (1 + storage cost) - convenience yield. Convenience yield = value of holding inventory (ability to respond to spot demand surges). High inventories = low convenience yield = contango. Low inventories = high convenience yield = backwardation. The convenience yield is observable: backwardation signals tight physical market (low inventories, high demand).

deepDive.heading: Oil Market Structure and OPEC Dynamics
deepDive.body: Oil is the most important commodity market (~$2T/year traded). Price determination: (1) Supply: OPEC+ production quotas (~40% of world supply); US shale (flexible, responds within 6 months to price signals); other producers. OPEC acts as a cartel — sets production to maintain target price range. Saudi Arabia is the swing producer with 2-3M bbl/day spare capacity; (2) Demand: transportation (50% of demand), petrochemicals, industrial. Relatively inelastic to price in the short run; (3) Inventories: US EIA weekly inventory data; IEA monthly; OPEC monthly. Inventory changes are the key short-term price signal. WTI vs. Brent: WTI (West Texas Intermediate) = US oil benchmark, traded at CME. Brent = North Sea benchmark, global reference for ~65% of world's oil. Spread between WTI and Brent reflects US pipeline constraints, export capacity. 2008-2014: WTI at discount to Brent due to US pipeline bottlenecks (Cushing, Oklahoma). Post-2015 US export lift: spread narrowed. Oil market regimes: (1) OPEC discipline: high price, low supply volatility; (2) Price war (2014-16, 2020): Saudi Arabia floods market to punish US shale / Russia. Price collapses; (3) Demand shock (2020 COVID): demand falls 30% in weeks; oil went negative (-$37) as storage exhausted; WTI May contract settlement was -$37.63 on April 20, 2020 — unique event from futures mechanics. [1]

deepDive.heading: Gold as a Monetary Asset
deepDive.body: Gold's unique properties: (1) No income: no dividends, no coupons, no rent. Return is pure price appreciation (or depreciation); (2) Indestructible: unlike oil or grain, gold is not consumed. Total above-ground gold stock ≈ 200,000 tonnes; mine supply adds ~3,500 tonnes/year (1.75%). Gold stock >> annual flow → price driven by marginal demand, not supply; (3) Store of value: used as money for 5,000 years; held by central banks (~35,000 tonnes, 17% of above-ground supply); (4) Crisis hedge: in financial crises, gold rises as investors flee counterparty risk; (5) Currency hedge: gold tends to rise when the USD weakens (dollar-denominated commodity → inverse relationship). Gold price drivers: (a) Real interest rates: gold opportunity cost is the real rate. When real rates fall (negative real rates common 2010-2021), gold rises. Gold/TIPS inverse relationship is the strongest observable driver; (b) USD strength: inverse; (c) Central bank buying/selling: net central bank purchases strongly correlated with gold price movements; (d) Investor demand (ETF flows): GLD ETF holdings as a proxy for institutional gold demand. Valuation: gold has no intrinsic DCF value. 'Fair value' models use real rate models, but gold can deviate significantly from model value. Gold stocks (GDX, GDXJ): amplified gold exposure (3-4× beta to gold price) with operational and country risk. [2]

deepDive.heading: Agricultural Commodities and Weather Risks
deepDive.body: Agricultural commodities: grains (corn, wheat, soybeans), soft commodities (coffee, cocoa, sugar, cotton), livestock (live cattle, lean hogs). Price determinants: (1) Weather and climate: El Niño/La Niña affect crop yields globally; drought, flood, late frost cause supply shocks; (2) Growing seasons: seasonality is fundamental — corn planted in spring, harvested in fall; Southern Hemisphere vs. Northern Hemisphere seasons offset; (3) Policy: US biofuels mandate (corn → ethanol); European CAP (Common Agricultural Policy) subsidies; export bans (India wheat export ban 2022); (4) Currency: commodities priced in USD; USD appreciation makes commodities cheaper in local currency, reducing demand from non-USD countries. Key agricultural futures: CME Group (CBOT): Corn (ZC), Soybean (ZS), Wheat (ZW), Live Cattle (LE), Lean Hogs (HE). ICE Futures: Coffee (KC), Sugar (SB), Cocoa (CC), Cotton (CT). Supply-demand balance reports: USDA WASDE (World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates): monthly report; major market-moving event. Weather models: NOAA 30/90-day forecasts; private weather services (DTN) used by commodity traders. Agricultural commodities display strong mean reversion over multi-year cycles: high prices incentivise more planting → supply surplus → price falls. Cobweb model: price oscillations around equilibrium due to production lag. [1]

takeaways: Commodity total return = spot return + roll yield + collateral yield; contango = negative roll; backwardation = positive roll. [1]
Commodity inflation beta ≈ 7-14; strongest short-run inflation hedge; equity inflation beta ≈ -0.5 to 0. [1]
Gold: driven by real interest rates (inverse), USD strength (inverse), central bank demand, and crisis demand. [2]
Oil: OPEC swing production + US shale supply + weekly EIA inventory data = three key price drivers. [1]
Agriculture: weather, USDA WASDE reports, and currency are primary short-term drivers; mean-reversion over cycles. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why did WTI crude oil trade at -$37 in April 2020 and what does that mean?
edgeCases.a: On April 20, 2020, the WTI May 2020 futures contract settled at -$37.63/barrel — the first negative oil price in history. The mechanism: (1) Physical delivery requirement: WTI futures require delivery of physical oil at Cushing, Oklahoma. Traders holding WTI futures must either take physical delivery or roll to the next month before expiry; (2) Storage exhaustion: COVID-19 demand destruction (global oil demand fell 30% in April 2020). Cushing storage capacity ≈ 80M barrels was almost entirely filled; (3) Forced selling: traders who couldn't store oil (no storage contracts) faced the prospect of receiving oil with nowhere to put it. They had to pay someone to take the contract — hence negative price; (4) Financial traders caught short: many retail ETF investors held USO (United States Oil Fund), which was rolling May contracts at the worst possible time. USO was forced to sell May contracts into a market with no buyers; (5) Brent crude: only fell to $25 (not negative) — Brent is cash-settled, no physical delivery, no Cushing storage problem. Lesson: oil futures are physically settled instruments with real-world storage constraints. Financial instrument price can disconnect from economic value when physical market mechanics dominate. The -$37 price was a settlement price, not a tradeable price for most market participants. Post-crisis: CFTC and exchanges added position limits for physically-settled futures near expiry to prevent recurrence.

edgeCases.q: How should institutional investors think about commodity allocation sizing?
edgeCases.a: Commodity allocation framework: (1) Inflation scenario: if the primary concern is inflation risk (e.g., pension fund with nominal liability and inflation-sensitive spending), commodities merit 5-15% allocation. The inflation beta provides insurance. In disinflation scenarios (post-2008, 2013-2021), commodity allocations were heavily reduced; (2) Diversification benefit: correlations are unstable. Commodities diversify well in inflation/supply-shock environments but not in demand-shock crises (2008, 2020 both saw commodity drawdowns alongside equities). Dynamic allocation: increase commodity weight when inflation breakevens rise; decrease when disinflation dominates; (3) Roll yield environment: check the term structure before investing. If energy futures are in steep contango (S&P GSCI lost 40%+ of spot price gains to roll costs 2005-2020), the total return is far below spot return. Prefer commodity indices with roll yield optimisation (Bloomberg Commodity Index roll method, or tactical roll across the term structure); (4) Vehicle choice: physically-backed ETF (gold, silver): no roll cost. Futures-based ETF: significant roll drag for contango commodities. Managed futures/CTA: optimal rolling, can go short, higher information ratio than passive long; (5) Position sizing: the Sharpe ratio of commodity futures is low (0.2-0.4) due to volatility and roll drag. Size accordingly relative to equities and bonds in a mean-variance framework. Kelly criterion suggests modest sizing.

sources.label: Gorton, G. & Rouwenhorst, K.G. (2006) — Facts and Fantasies about Commodity Futures, FAJ
Erb, C.B. & Harvey, C.R. (2013) — The Golden Dilemma, FAJ
